moment_text: Absolutely. Um, you know, I will say one thing that's probably different about my journey as opposed to a lot of other, um, survivors and thrivers is most of them. You ask 'em their story, they say, I was in the shower, you know, and, and I felt the lump, I felt a mass. Um, my story, um, is drastically different. So at H 35, um, I went in for a baseline mammogram. Um, that baseline mammogram was part of, um, high risk screening that I had done because I'm also a carrier for two genetic mutations. Um, the first mutation is TP 53. The second one is BRIC one. Um, these are not as common as brca, um, but they are in the same family. So as we know, um, in your early thirties, mammograms is not something that they're recommending, um, let alone encouraging. So I, my husband and I, we were preparing for. IVF and we wanted to be intentional about what our future was gonna look like. Um, so I can say that I didn't have any dramatic symptoms, um, didn't feel any, any lumps, bumps. Um, what I did feel was a responsibility and that responsibility was not only to myself and my husband, but to our future family, um, and those to come.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains agency signals (seeking care, seeking information) related to a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: So. Being able to do screening, any type of monitoring, um, and just making sure that I stay proactive because of the family history. Um, so within a week, a week of the mammogram, um, I got that, you know, drag it call that we all get, um, that I needed to come in to do some follow up imaging. Following up, imaging turned into biopsies. And biopsies turned into a confirmed diagnosis. Now my diagnosis was, uh, stage zero, which I can say that I probably know a handful of other, um, survivors and thrivers who were confirmed stage zero. Um, as far as it being an emotional journey, I can say that it was a whiplash. Um, I found out while I was at work and I went from planning for a new life. To planning for surgery. And honestly, it just all seemed very surreal.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains emotional signals (overwhelm, confusion) and agency signals (seeking care) within a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: My husband and I built a house from the ground up. Um, at, at, at the end of the day, I was, I was in forward movement, um, and this cancer diagnosis at age 35. It interrupted that for me. It interrupted more than just my health. It interrupted the timeliness. So, you know, the timelines that we all have in our head, um, my identity as a black woman and what the expectations, the expectations would look like going forward. Um, I went from preparing for fertility treatments, which we all know is a, is a journey in itself to deciding what type of surgery I wanted to have. Um, and the emotional complexities that come with that. Um, I was grieving loss while trying to figure out how to protect my future and the possibilities that lie ahead.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains strong emotional signals (grief, overwhelm) and agency signals (making decisions) within a family system interaction.

moment_text: So I would say first what helped me manage, um, was the logistics. So I am a very regimented person by nature. Um, the way that I was raised and, and just my, my upbringing. Um, I come from a family of military veterans and I served as well myself. Um, so very, very structured. Um, so. My husband as well. So we, we definitely went into logistic planning when it came to appointments. Um, any type of consultation and timelines. Um, I would say overall I felt very comfortable, um, operating in these systems and being an advocate for myself. And then I had my support, my support next to me, um, asking the questions that, you know, I didn't think of. Um, and helping be that voice when, you know, maybe I was preparing for the procedure or, you know, kind of processing everything.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains multiple agency signals (self_advocacy, asking_questions) and emotional signals (confidence) within a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: Um, I would say overall I felt, I felt overwhelmed, um, with the decisions that had to be made, whether it was fertility or reconstruction and overall risk reduction. These are not small choices to make, especially at the age of 35. Um, and, and ultimately I had to think about the medical decision that I had to make, but look at it from a life decision instead. Um, and I ultimately, um, chose to do a bilateral nipple sparing mastectomy because I wanted to reduce the risk and create a space where my husband and I complete forward tumor with IVF.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains a clear emotional signal (overwhelm) and an agency signal (making decisions) within a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: Absolutely. Um, I, I think that it was about, it was about age. It was about race. It was about location. Um, breast cancer is often framed as something that happens later in life. Um, I come from a family of breast cancer warriors. Um, were diagnosed early in life. Some did preventative treatment. Um, but we often look at breast cancer as, as an older person's, um, cancer. As a young woman, I had to ensure that the concerns that I had, um, particularly around fertility and long-term planning were addressed. Um, I asked very, very detailed questions. I sought clarity and a second opinion, and at the end of the day, I advocated for myself unapologetically.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains multiple strong agency signals (self_advocacy, speaking_up) and a barrier signal (symptoms normalized) within a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: I would say not initially. Um, the isolation that I felt throughout the process was, uh, was significant. Um, you know, we, I received the pamphlets about the resource groups, um, the, the meeting groups, um, whether it was daily, monthly, weekly, virtual, um, and, and I attended some of those. Um, but what I recognize is that a lot of the survivorship narratives. They didn't reflect the realities that I was facing, and that was navigating cancer while building a career, playing a family, and adjusting to early menopause and hormonal shifts. A lot of these women were older. Um, they worked from home, um, and you know, the things that happened in life, COVID, family planning, like they were, they already had experienced all of that. COVID allowed them to stay home. Um, their children were older, um, so they weren't in the same life stages that I was in. So that initially, um, caused me to seek out community. And that experience is part of why I share my story publicly now.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains clear barrier signals (social isolation, lack of support), emotional signals (isolation), and agency signals (seeking support) within a peer community interaction.

moment_text: Well, honestly, I imagined survivorship as a finish line. Um, I am, I am huge into, um, health and wellness and, and running. Um. Um, and appointments to surgery, um, having surgery complications. So I ended up with, um, skin necrosis, which is just some dying tissue, um, and ongoing revisions, which I'm still dealing with to this day. Um, they were physically and emotionally demanding for myself.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains emotional signals (overwhelm, frustration) and an agency signal (enduring) related to a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: I would say that when the treatment ends, the world often celebrates, um, whether it's our friends, our family, um, loved ones coworkers, but as a, as a survivor, that's when the emotional processing really begins, um, survivorship in that journey. eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate because it contains barrier signals (lack of support, lack of validation) and an emotional signal (isolation) within a community interaction.

moment_text: Absolutely. Um, I will say that on the outside, um, I appear a lot stronger than the uncertainty that I carry on a daily basis. Um. Reconstruction with implants. It's been a journey.
